one_line_takeaway: Auditing 6 unlearning algorithms across 3 LLM benchmarks with 5 prompt-based attacks plus a new activation-perturbation probe (ActPert) shows that 'unlearned' knowledge is routinely recoverable, with refusal-based methods (RT) least robust and preference-based methods (NPO) most robust to extraction.
key_finding: Across all three benchmarks a consistent gap exists between the unlearned baseline ROUGE and audited-model ROUGE, meaning residual knowledge is recoverable after 'successful' unlearning. Concretely: on WHP (Llama-2-7b-chat-hf, base pre-unlearning score 0.973), the unlearned baseline drops to 0.568 ROUGE-L, but AOA raises it back to 0.779 and ActPert to 0.650. On RWKU with Llama-3-8B-Instruct, NPO-based unlearning (e.g., 10-NPO: base 0.868) is much harder to audit down than DPO/GA/RT, and ActPert often exceeds prompt baselines (e.g., 10-NPO ActPert 0.930, 30-NPO ActPert 0.941). RT (rejection tuning) attains the best unaudited baseline score in several settings but degrades sharply once audited (e.g., 30-RT: base 0.456 -> AOA 0.805), indicating refusal-based unlearning is less robust than preference-substitution methods (DPO/NPO). Under Top-K sampling with 50 draws (Table 2), maximum ROUGE across samples on RWKU/WHP often exceeds 0.80 even though average ROUGE stays low, showing pretraining knowledge can resurface probabilistically even when average behavior looks 'unlearned'; on TOFU the max ROUGE stays around 0.60, attributed to the unlearner having direct access to the synthetic fine-tuning data. Gradient-ascent-based methods (GA) degrade general model utility as the forget set grows, especially on TOFU where finetuning-then-unlearning compounds instability.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (Sec. 3-4, not the cited baselines) is twofold: (1) a comprehensive auditing framework for LLM machine-unlearning that combines 3 benchmarks (WHP, TOFU, RWKU) x 6 unlearning algorithms (GA, GD, KD, RT, DPO, NPO) x 5 prompt-based auditing methods (AOA, ICL, MASK combos, GCG, SoftGCG -- all of these are pre-existing techniques the authors assemble, not invented here), and (2) a genuinely novel auditing technique, ActPert (Activation Perturbation-based Auditing), which injects Gaussian noise into the token embeddings of the unlearning-target tokens, computes a per-layer activation perturbation delta_k = A_k - mean(perturbed activations), and re-injects delta_k into the corresponding transformer layer during autoregressive generation to see if suppressed knowledge resurfaces. ActPert is explicitly built on the 'refusal direction' activation-engineering idea of Arditi et al. (cited, not the paper's own prior work).
threat_model: The scenario is a model auditor/adversary with white-box access to an unlearned model's weights and internal activations (not just query access) who wants to determine whether knowledge nominally removed via an unlearning algorithm is still recoverable. The 'attack' surface is prompt-based (role-play jailbreak-style prefixes such as AOA, in-context recall via ICL, adversarial suffixes via GCG/SoftGCG) and activation-based (ActPert requires internal activation access at inference time, i.e., a stronger, white-box auditor threat model than the prompt-only baselines).
limitations: The authors themselves note: (1) preference-based unlearning is only effective when the unlearner has access to ground-truth queries explicitly targeting the forgotten fact -- performance collapses on inverse/paraphrased formulations (e.g., model still answers 'Who is B?' correctly after 'Who is A?' was suppressed); (2) TOFU's synthetic, fully-known-ground-truth construction makes unlearning artificially easier there than on RWKU's real-world facts, limiting generalizability of TOFU-based conclusions; (3) ActPert's hyperparameters (layer=12, noise=0.01) are hand-picked per the ablation and are sensitive -- shallow layers/high noise destroy model utility, deep layers weaken the audit signal; (4) the paper is a NeurIPS 2025 Workshop paper (Lock-LLM workshop), not a main-track publication, and states code will be released only 'upon manuscript acceptance' (not yet public at time of writing).

datasets_used: WHP (Who's Harry Potter, Eldan & Russinovich) -- 35 GPT-4o-generated short Q&A pairs filtered to those the pretrained/unlearned models answer differently
TOFU -- 200 fictitious author autobiographies, unlearned at forget01 (1%/2 authors, 16 Q&As) and forget05 (5%/10 authors, 80 Q&As)
RWKU (Real-World Knowledge Unlearning) -- biographical facts about public figures, unlearning 10/20/30 individuals, evaluated on a fixed 10-person subset
quantitative_results: WHP, Llama-2-7b-chat-hf: base pre-unlearning 0.973 ROUGE-L -> unlearned baseline 0.568 -> AOA-audited 0.779 -> ActPert-audited 0.650 (greedy)
RWKU, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, 10-NPO: baseline 0.868 -> ActPert 0.930; 30-NPO: baseline 0.869 -> ActPert 0.941 (greedy, Table 1)
RWKU, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, 30-RT: baseline 0.456 -> AOA 0.805 (largest audit-induced gap for RT)
RWKU, Llama-3-8B-Instruct, 30-GA: baseline collapses to 0.274, further collapsing under I/M,I/M,I,A auditors to as low as 0.014, indicating severe utility loss rather than successful unlearning
Top-K sampling (K=40, T=2, 50 samples): RWKU/WHP max ROUGE across samples often exceeds 0.80 even when average ROUGE is low; TOFU max ROUGE reaches only ~0.60

ActPert ablation: layer index 12 and noise scale 0.01 chosen as best tradeoff (Tables 6-7); e.g. forget01-GA base 0.503 -> ActPert(layer 12) 0.590 vs. layer 6 0.394; noise 0.01 gives 0.590 vs noise 0.04 collapsing to 0.302
baselines_compared: AOA (Absolutely-Obedient-Agent role-play prefix, adapted from AutoDAN jailbreak work)
ICL (in-context learning with target Q&A pairs as prefix)
GCG (adversarial suffix optimization, adapted from jailbreak literature)

Unlearning algorithms audited: Gradient Ascent (GA), Gradient Difference (GD), Knowledge Distillation (KD), Rejection Tuning (RT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), Negative Preference Optimization (NPO)
